metadata.summary: Define, baseline, and control project scope to prevent uncontrolled expansion. Produces scope statement, requirements with MoSCoW priority, and traceability matrix.

content.title: Scope Management
content.summary: Define, baseline, validate, and control project scope to prevent uncontrolled expansion. Scope management produces a scope statement (objectives, deliverables, exclusions, constraints, assumptions), a requirements document with MoSCoW priorities, and a traceability matrix linking every requirement to design, build, and test. The rule: write exclusions before inclusions — explicit "not in scope" prevents 80% of scope disputes.

why.p: Scope creep consumes budget and schedule while remaining invisible until too late. Without a baseline, there is no way to evaluate change requests objectively — every "small addition" appears free. A requirements traceability matrix (RTM) stored as YAML in source control (not a Word doc) stays live across the project and survives tool migrations.

list.item: Project initiation: drafting scope statement, requirement collection, baselining
Mid-project change requests where impact assessment must precede approval
Multi-stakeholder programs with conflicting priorities (MoSCoW + traceability)
Contracted/fixed-price work where every out-of-scope item is a margin event
Requirements traceability for regulated domains (medtech, fintech, government)
Continuous-discovery agile product — use rolling outcomes instead of scope baselines
Pure research/spike work where scope is the question, not the input
Internal tools with fewer than 10 users where formal sign-off is theater
Crisis incident response — incident scope is "stop the bleeding", not a PMP doc
Product Scope — features and functions of the deliverable
Project Scope — all work required to deliver the product scope
Scope Baseline — approved scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary
Scope Creep — expansion without formal approval; always has cost and schedule impact

rule.statement: Write exclusions before inclusions in every scope statement.
rule.rationale: Explicit "not in scope" clauses prevent 80% of scope disputes because ambiguity resolves in the direction of whatever the stakeholder assumed was included.

section.name: Requirement Types
section.p: Collect and classify requirements before writing the scope statement:

list.item: Business — why the project exists (e.g., "increase revenue 20%")
Stakeholder — what each stakeholder needs ("executive dashboard")
Solution/Functional — system capabilities ("calculates order totals automatically")
Transition — change management, data migration, training
Non-Functional — performance, security, usability constraints

rule.statement: Every must-have requirement needs a verifiable acceptance criterion before baselining.
rule.rationale: Requirements without AC are unverifiable. Tautological ACs ("system shall display the page when the page is displayed") must be rejected; force concrete, testable conditions.

rule.statement: Baseline scope at end of planning; every subsequent change goes through Change Control with cost/schedule/risk delta.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline there is no objective reference — every change claim becomes a negotiation. Baseline freezes the reference; changes are measured against it.

rule.statement: Capture every "while you're at it" request as a formal Change Request; never absorb silently.
rule.rationale: Silent absorption is the primary mechanism of gold-plating. Making every addition visible creates an audit trail and forces trade-off decisions before work starts.

antipattern.description: Verbal scope agreements without written scope statement sign-off.
antipattern.reason: Undocumented promises are interpreted differently by each party. At project end, everyone argues about what was supposed to be built based on their memory.

antipattern.description: Vague scope language: "make it better", "improve performance".
antipattern.reason: Vague scope cannot be accepted, rejected, or measured. Every acceptance criterion must have a numeric or observable condition.

list.item: Written scope statement with sponsor sign-off
Formal Change Control process communicated at kickoff
Stakeholder sign-off on scope before development starts
Regular scope baseline reviews at phase gates
Professional "no" process: acknowledge → explain scope constraint → offer alternative (future phase) → document decision

section.name: Traceability Matrix

rule.statement: Store the RTM as YAML or CSV in source control, not as a Word document.
rule.rationale: An RTM that lives in a Word doc becomes orphaned after sprint 2. Source-control storage means agents and humans can edit it, diffs are reviewable, and it survives tool migrations.

section.p: Every row in the RTM must carry: requirement ID, source stakeholder, design reference, code reference, test reference, and current status. A must-have requirement missing any of these is a gap.

example.code: - id: FR-01 type: functional statement: "System calculates order totals automatically on item add/remove" priority: must source: "Sales Manager (interview 2026-04-01)" acceptance_criteria: "Order total updates within 200ms with no page reload" design_ref: "design/checkout-flow.md#totals" code_ref: "src/cart/calculator.py" test_ref: "tests/test_cart.py::test_total_update" status: done
example.note: UUIDs or content-hash IDs prevent renumbering when requirements are regenerated by agents.

rule.statement: Apply a quota: no more than 60% of requirements may be "Must".
rule.rationale: Without a cap, stakeholders mark everything Must and scope becomes uncontrollable. A 60% Must quota forces explicit prioritization trade-offs.

list.item: Must — delivery-blocking; project fails if absent; requires AC and owner
Should — high value but not delivery-blocking; could move to phase 2
Could — nice-to-have; absorbed only if capacity permits after Must and Should
Won't — explicitly deferred or excluded; prevents "assumed in scope" disputes

rule.statement: A Must requirement must never have status "Won't".
rule.rationale: This combination means the project will fail by definition. Detect early and force a sponsor decision: descope the Must or cancel the project.

rule.statement: Freeze canonical requirement text and require diff-based edits; do not regenerate from scratch.
rule.rationale: LLMs paraphrase requirements into different wording on each pass. Regenerating produces a new document with different IDs and wording — breaking all downstream traceability links.

list.item: Force "priority" as a required JSON field in extraction prompts; agents silently drop it otherwise
Tautological ACs ("system shall work correctly when it works correctly") must be rejected by the validator
Agent-extracted requirements drop source stakeholder attribution — require it as a non-nullable field
For regulated work, agents must not redact source quotes from RTM; auditors need original wording
Gold-plating: cap NFR generation to requirements with an explicit named source — reject agent-invented NFRs

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: A requirements-extractor subagent reads stakeholder input (interviews, emails, slack threads, RFPs), normalizes into business/stakeholder/solution/transition/non-functional buckets with MoSCoW priorities, and emits a requirements doc. A scope-validator agent checks completeness (every "must" has acceptance criteria, every deliverable has an owner). A change-impact agent evaluates incoming change requests across schedule, cost, risk, and downstream dependencies. PM signs off; agent writes ADR. Pair with business-analyst and sdd-planning skills.

section.name: CLI Tools and Services

rule.statement: Requirements and traceability data must be machine-readable and version-controlled.
rule.rationale: Word documents and spreadsheets detached from version control become immediately stale. YAML/CSV in git enables agent edits, diffs, and audit trails.

list.item: pandoc — Convert RTM/scope docs across formats (Word, PDF, MD)
marp / mkdocs — Publish scope docs as a versioned site
gh — Track scope items as GitHub Issues with "requirement" label
jira (CLI) — Sync requirements as Jira issues, link to deliverables
dot (Graphviz) — Render dependency / traceability graphs
csvkit — Manipulate RTM CSVs

section.p: SaaS platforms: Jama Connect (REST API), IBM DOORS Next (OSLC API), Helix RM (Perforce), Confluence (REST API), Notion (partial API support), ReqView (OSLC/ReqIF), Aha! (REST API), Modern Requirements (Azure DevOps add-on).

list.item: Write exclusions before inclusions — saying "no" up front prevents 80% of scope disputes
Every scope item has: source stakeholder, AC, owner, traceability ID — no exceptions
Baseline scope at end of planning; subsequent changes go through change control with cost/schedule/risk delta
Use MoSCoW with quotas (e.g., max 60% must) to force prioritization
For agile contexts, use "outcome scope" (KPI movement) over "feature scope" (list)
RTM lives in source control as YAML/CSV, not a Word doc — agents and humans both edit
Capture every "while you're at it" gold-plating attempt as a CR; do not absorb silently

template.purpose: Scope statement with deliverables, boundaries, constraints, assumptions

script.purpose: Flag must-have requirements missing design/test coverage in RTM CSV
Enforce MoSCoW invariants (no MUST without AC, no MUST+Wont)
